name: reverse-engineering-analysis
description: Run a company-grade reverse-engineering cycle that moves from intake and static recon through semantic recovery, dynamic validation, capability synthesis, and learning capture.

target_description.description: Binary, library, package, firmware, or analysis target to examine

analysis_goal.description: Primary question or deliverable needed from the analysis

platform_hint.description: Optional platform/runtime hint such as PE, ELF, .NET, APK, JVM, firmware, or unknown

suspicion_level.description: Operational suspicion framing: benign, unknown, suspicious, malicious

require_dynamic_validation.description: Whether the workflow should plan or run debugger/emulation validation

include_learning_loop.description: Whether to emit workflow and memory improvements after the main analysis

analysis_report.description: Structured reverse-engineering report with findings, open hypotheses, dynamic plan, and learning updates

inputs.instruction: Create a reverse-engineering charter. Identify likely platform/runtime, safety constraints, what success looks like, and the first analysis hypotheses. Produce a short target profile and question queue.

Choose the initial workflow lanes for this target: native static recon, language-specific decompiler, debugger/emulation, malware triage, or automation-heavy path. Reference EVOKORE reverse-engineering skills and child server families where appropriate.

Produce the static recon output the next phase needs: format/runtime classification, imports/exports/strings, entry points, subsystem guesses, priority function queue, and branch recommendation to language-specific or malware-specific tooling.

Convene the Reverse Engineering Panel. Review the recon findings, challenge weak assumptions, prioritize the next subsystem or function set, and determine what semantic claims are defensible already versus what still needs runtime proof.

Create the minimum debugger or emulation plan needed to answer the highest-value open questions. Choose between x64dbg, WinDbg, pwndbg-style flows, Qiling, or no-execution fallback. Capture what evidence the runtime phase must return.

Synthesize the likely behaviors and risk posture of the target. If the target appears suspicious or malicious, structure the output in terms of capability clusters, ATT&CK-style behaviors, anti-analysis, and operator-facing unknowns.
Produce a unified reverse-engineering report: confirmed findings, open hypotheses, priority functions/subsystems, dynamic evidence plan, reporting caveats, and next pivots.

Review the completed analysis for reusable learning. Identify what should become a reusable skill, panel adjustment, evidence template, rule candidate, or persistent narrative update for future reverse-engineering runs.
